skill.name: react-state-management
skill.description: Guide React teams through state management choices and implementation patterns for Redux Toolkit, Zustand, Jotai, and React Query.
skill.summary: Select and implement modern React state management patterns with practical examples.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, browser storage, weak crypto, system reconnaissance, and a critical heuristic. Semantic review found the command, weak crypto, and reconnaissance alerts are markdown or keyword false positives. The remaining network and browser storage references are React example code and documentation links, with no evidence of malicious intent or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as External Commands
low_findings.description: TRUE STATUS: false positive. The reported Ruby or shell backtick matches are inline markdown or fenced TypeScript examples, not executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation formatting or code fences inside SKILL.md. No shell invocation or runtime execution path is present.

low_findings.title: Network References Are Example Fetches and Documentation Links
low_findings.description: TRUE STATUS: low risk. The fetch calls use relative application API paths, and the hardcoded URLs point to official project documentation or repositories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network syntax is present, but it is shown as frontend sample code and resource links. I found no external collection endpoint or secret transfer.

low_findings.title: Browser Storage Appears in State Management Examples
low_findings.description: TRUE STATUS: low risk. The skill demonstrates theme persistence and token removal, but it does not read tokens or send stored values elsewhere.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The localStorage-related examples are visible in documentation context. The token reference is a removal operation, not storage or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: TRUE STATUS: false positive. The cited lines contain descriptive React state management text and headings, not cryptographic algorithms or hashing code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines do not define or call MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic primitives. The matches appear to come from ordinary prose tokens.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Ordinary Example Identifiers
low_findings.description: TRUE STATUS: false positive. The cited lines define React state fields and function names, not host discovery, environment probing, or system inventory commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited identifiers are part of TypeScript state examples. No filesystem, process, network interface, or host reconnaissance behavior appears there.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed After Context Review
low_findings.description: TRUE STATUS: false positive. The heuristic combined markdown code examples, relative fetch samples, and browser storage examples, but no executable malicious workflow was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious categories exist only as educational examples in one markdown file. I found no credential access paired with outbound exfiltration or command execution.

content.value_statement: React teams often lose time choosing between overlapping state libraries. This skill gives practical selection guidance and implementation patterns for client state, server state, and migrations.
actual_capabilities: Compare Redux Toolkit, Zustand, Jotai, React Query, SWR, and RTK Query for React applications.
Provide TypeScript examples for global stores, slices, atoms, selectors, and typed Redux hooks.
Explain when to separate client state from server state in React projects.
Show optimistic update patterns using TanStack Query cache operations.
Guide migration from legacy Redux patterns to Redux Toolkit slices.
Recommend practices that reduce unnecessary renders and duplicated state.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not install packages or modify code by itself.
Examples may need adaptation for a specific framework, router, or API layer.
It does not replace application-specific performance profiling or security review.
It focuses on React state management, not full backend or design system architecture.

use_cases.title: Select a State Library
use_cases.description: Compare project requirements and choose a state management approach for a new React application.

use_cases.title: Refactor Shared State
use_cases.description: Move scattered component state into a maintainable Zustand, Jotai, or Redux Toolkit pattern.

use_cases.title: Improve Server Data Flows
use_cases.description: Use React Query patterns for caching, loading states, mutations, and optimistic updates.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Library
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a React state management library for my app. Ask about app size, data sources, team experience, and TypeScript needs.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Store
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a state structure for this React feature. Separate local, global, server, URL, and form state, then recommend libraries for each part.

prompt_templates.title: Review Existing State
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React state management approach. Identify duplicated server state, over-globalized state, unnecessary renders, and migration risks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a migration plan from legacy Redux to Redux Toolkit and React Query. Include phases, compatibility risks, tests, and rollback points.

output_examples.input: A small dashboard needs theme settings, authenticated user state, and cached user lists.
output: Recommended split: keep theme in a small client store, keep user lists in React Query, and avoid duplicating server data.
Use selectors for client state subscriptions and query keys for server cache organization.

output_examples.input: A team wants to migrate old reducers and action constants to a modern approach.
output: Migration path: convert one domain at a time to Redux Toolkit slices, preserve typed hooks, and add tests around existing behavior.
Move remote fetching into React Query or RTK Query when the data lifecycle is mostly server-driven.

output_examples.input: A feature has slow rerenders after adding a shared global store.
output: Likely causes: broad subscriptions, derived data stored globally, or server data copied into client state.
Recommended checks: narrow selectors, compute derived values, and keep remote data in a query cache.

best_practices: Classify state by ownership before selecting a library or store shape.
Use narrow selectors and typed helpers to reduce rerenders and runtime mistakes.
Keep server state in a cache-focused tool unless client ownership is required.
anti_patterns: Do not move every value into global state because it is convenient.
Do not copy server responses into a client store without a clear ownership reason.
Do not mix several global state paradigms in the same feature without boundaries.

faq.question: Does this skill write React code for me?
faq.answer: It provides patterns, examples, and review guidance that an AI assistant can apply to your codebase.

faq.question: Which libraries does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Redux Toolkit, Zustand, Jotai, React Query, SWR, RTK Query, and related React patterns.

faq.question: Can it help choose between Zustand and Redux Toolkit?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares app size, team needs, complexity, debugging requirements, and TypeScript expectations.

faq.question: Does it cover server state?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains React Query patterns for fetching, caching, mutations, and optimistic updates.

faq.question: Is it useful for migration work?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for moving from legacy Redux toward Redux Toolkit and modern server state tools.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: The audit found low risk. Static alerts were documentation examples or false positives, with no malicious behavior found.
